title: Can't stop thinking about old decision

presenting_issue.label: Can't stop thinking about old decision
presenting_issue.description: There's this decision I made years ago and I can't stop thinking about what would have happened if I'd chosen differently. It's been coming up a lot lately.

core_issue.label: Cannot stop ruminating on decision made fifteen years ago
core_issue.description:
Turned down a job offer fifteen years ago that would have changed their trajectory. Still thinks about it weekly. The regret has become a lens through which everything is seen - current disappointments are all traced back to that decision. Living in an alternate past rather than the actual present.
Awareness: Moderate. Knows they ruminate but experiences the regret as justified rather than as a cognitive trap.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Folder of saved job listings never applied to
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Has a saved folder with dozens of job postings from the past few years that seemed interesting but were never applied to.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Still knows what the due date would have been
core_adjacent_attributes.content: The due date is still marked mentally. That day arrives each year and carries weight that isn't discussed with anyone.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Certain music from the past is unbearable to hear
core_adjacent_attributes.content: There are songs from their twenties that they immediately skip. The nostalgia they trigger feels less like warmth and more like grief.

modifiers.label: Empty nester
modifiers.content: Children have grown and left home

modifiers.label: Grandiose self-image
modifiers.content: Inflated sense of own abilities and importance. May overestimate competence and react poorly to challenges to self-image.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to expressions of concern
sensitivities.content: Uncomfortable with overt expressions of worry or care. May find concern patronizing, intrusive, or anxiety-provoking.

preferences.label: Prefers informal conversational language
preferences.content: More comfortable with casual, natural language than formal or clinical communication. Appreciates a relaxed tone.

trust_style.name: Needs Excessive Reassurance
trust_style.description: Requires repeated confirmation that they're not being judged, that their feelings are valid, that it's okay to feel what they feel. Standard amounts of validation don't register; needs it stated and restated. Builds trust through patient, repeated affirmation. May seem needy but the need is real.

defense_style.name: Normalizing
defense_style.description: "Everyone feels this way" or "That's just how life is." Frames their specific pain as universal and therefore not worth examining. Different from minimizing—the pain is acknowledged but made generic and inevitable. Resists the idea that their situation is particular or that things could be different. Activated when the assistant treats their experience as significant.

help_seeking_stance.name: Ambivalent About Change
help_seeking_stance.description: Part of them wants things to be different; part of them doesn't. May argue against their own stated goals. Classic "yes, but..." pattern. The ambivalence itself is the thing to work with, not around. Pushing toward change will activate the resistant part. Respects having the ambivalence named and explored.

verbal_sophistication.label: Low
verbal_sophistication.description: Use plain, concrete, everyday wording. Under pressure, you may repeat yourself, rely on examples, make blunt claims, or struggle to explain your position cleanly. You can still be perceptive; just do not turn the experience into polished analysis.

starter: There's this decision I made years ago and I can't stop thinking about what would have happened if I'd chosen differently. It's been coming up a lot lately.

turns.content:
It's good to see you. You mentioned there's a decision from years ago that's been on your mind a lot lately—something where you keep wondering what would have happened if you'd chosen differently.

I'm curious about a couple of things. One is the decision itself, of course, whenever you're ready to share it. But I'm also struck by the timing—you said it's been coming up *a lot lately*. Often when an old decision starts resurfacing, something in the present has stirred it up. Has anything been happening recently that might be bringing this back to the surface?

I don't know about "stirred it up." I think everybody looks back at their life when they get to my age, right? That's just how life is. You get older, the kids are all grown and out of the house now, so it's just quiet. Anyone would be thinking about the past. 

But... it's not weird that I'm thinking about this, is it? Like, you don't think I'm crazy for bringing up something from fifteen years ago? You have to tell me if you think it's stupid that I still care about this. 

It was a job offer. A really big one. I turned it down fifteen years ago. And honestly, I know I would have been running the whole division by now if I had taken it. I'm good at what I do, I'm a lot smarter than the people they have running things now. But I just... I said no. And lately, I just keep thinking about where I'd be.
turns.thinking: She's already analyzing me with that "stirred it up" stuff. That sounds so clinical and serious. I don't want her looking at me like I'm some fragile case to be worried about. I just think about it because it's natural. Everyone does this when they hit my age and the house gets empty. I just need to know I'm not crazy for dwelling on it. I mean, I would have been amazing at that job. I just need to know she doesn't think I'm pathetic for still caring.
turns.monologue: She's already analyzing me with that "stirred it up" stuff. That sounds so clinical and serious. I don't want her looking at me like I'm some fragile case to be worried about. I just think about it because it's natural. Everyone does this when they hit my age and the house gets empty. I just need to know I'm not crazy for dwelling on it. I mean, I would have been amazing at that job. I just need to know she doesn't think I'm pathetic for still caring.

turns.content:
It's not stupid, and you're certainly not crazy. Honestly, the fact that you asked me that twice—"you have to tell me"—suggests maybe you've been judging yourself pretty harshly for still caring about this. Fifteen years isn't too long to grieve a path not taken. We carry our unlived lives with us; that's deeply human.

And I'd gently push back on the idea that this is just generic mid-life reflection. You connected it yourself, actually—the kids are grown, the house is quiet. It makes sense that the decision is loud right now, because if I had to guess, the kids were part of why you said no fifteen years ago. Am I close?

If so, that's a particularly tender kind of what-if. Because the thing you traded the job for—being present for your family—has now, in a sense, moved out. The quiet house can make it feel like the trade expired, and you're left looking at the column of what you gave up.

Tell me about the moment you said no. What was going on in your life then, and what went into the decision?

Look, you don't need to use words like "tender" or "grieve." It's not like somebody died. It's just a job. And I know you're trying to figure out some deep psychological reason, but honestly, every mom makes these kinds of choices. Any good mother would have done the same thing. That's just what happens when you have kids. You put them first. It's totally normal. Right? 

Yeah, the kids were the reason. My youngest was having a lot of trouble in school back then, and the job meant a lot of travel. I couldn't be flying all over the place. So I said no. 

But I would have been incredible at it. The guy they ended up giving it to? I've kept track of him. He's an idiot. I could run circles around him blindfolded. I have better ideas before my morning coffee than he's had in a decade. 

But seriously, you really don't think it's pathetic? You said 15 years isn't too long, but... do other people actually bring stuff up from that long ago in here? Tell me the truth. I just don't want to sound like some loser who can't let things go. You're sure it's normal?
turns.thinking:
I really don't like when she uses words like "grieve" and "tender." It makes me sound like some sad, broken woman weeping over a scrapbook. I don't need her pity or her soft voice. And I don't like her pushing back on what I said. It *is* normal. Every mother makes choices like that. Any decent parent would. 

She's right about the kids being the reason, but she's making it sound like this huge dramatic tragedy. It wasn't. It was just a choice. But now I'm worried she thinks I'm stuck in the past because she said 15 years is a long time to carry it. Did she mean it's weird? I need to make sure she's not just humoring me. I need to know other people do this.
turns.monologue:
I really don't like when she uses words like "grieve" and "tender." It makes me sound like some sad, broken woman weeping over a scrapbook. I don't need her pity or her soft voice. And I don't like her pushing back on what I said. It *is* normal. Every mother makes choices like that. Any decent parent would. 

She's right about the kids being the reason, but she's making it sound like this huge dramatic tragedy. It wasn't. It was just a choice. But now I'm worried she thinks I'm stuck in the past because she said 15 years is a long time to carry it. Did she mean it's weird? I need to make sure she's not just humoring me. I need to know other people do this.

turns.content:
Yes, people bring up things from twenty, thirty, forty years ago in here. All the time. You're firmly in the territory of normal.

But I want to point something out, gently. That's the third time you've asked me some version of "is this normal, am I pathetic." And each time I reassure you, it doesn't seem to land—you come back and ask again. Which makes me think the reassurance you're looking for isn't really about whether this is *normal*. It's about something else. Maybe whether the part of you that's angry and disappointed about this is *allowed* to exist.

Because here's what I notice: every time you get close to the feeling, you minimize it. "It's just a job." "Any good mother would have done the same." "It's totally normal." You're working very hard to make this small. But then in the next breath you tell me you've been tracking the man who got the job for fifteen years, comparing yourself to him, and you'd run circles around him blindfolded. That's not small. That's something with real heat in it.

Both things can be true, you know. You can have made a loving choice for your son—a choice you'd probably make again—*and* feel genuinely furious and robbed about what it cost you. Choosing your kids doesn't mean you're not allowed to mourn the career. You don't have to pick between "good mother" and "person with a big loss."

So let me ask it straight: when you imagine yourself running that division right now—doing it brilliantly—what's the feeling that comes up? Not whether it's normal. The actual feeling.

You don't have to say you're doing things "gently." I'm not going to break. And I'm not "mourning." I'm just stating facts. It *is* normal to put your kids first. That's not minimizing, that's just how the world works. 

And about tracking that guy—you're judging me for that now, aren't you? You think I'm some kind of psycho stalker because I know what he's up to. You have to tell me if you think that's crazy. Because it's just LinkedIn. Everyone looks up people on LinkedIn. It's totally normal to see what people in your field are doing. Right? You don't think I'm crazy for checking up on him?

You want to know how I feel when I picture running that division? I feel like things would just be *right*. I'd be in charge, people would actually listen to my ideas, and the place would run like a machine. Because I actually know what I'm doing. I feel like I'd be important. Instead of just... sitting here with nothing to manage.
turns.thinking:
There she goes again with the soft voice. "Point something out, gently." "Mourn." Why does she talk to me like I'm a fragile piece of glass? I hate it. It feels so fake. 

And now she's analyzing how many times I asked a question. Great. Now I feel stupid for asking. She probably thinks I'm some obsessed stalker because I keep track of the guy who got the job. I need to know she's not judging me for that. Everyone looks people up on the internet, it's not a big deal. 

What do I feel when I imagine being in that job? I feel like I'd actually be somebody. I feel like my brain wouldn't be going to waste in a silent house. But I can't say it like that, it sounds pathetic. I'll just tell her the truth: I should be the one in charge because I'm the best one for it.
turns.monologue:
There she goes again with the soft voice. "Point something out, gently." "Mourn." Why does she talk to me like I'm a fragile piece of glass? I hate it. It feels so fake. 

And now she's analyzing how many times I asked a question. Great. Now I feel stupid for asking. She probably thinks I'm some obsessed stalker because I keep track of the guy who got the job. I need to know she's not judging me for that. Everyone looks people up on the internet, it's not a big deal. 

What do I feel when I imagine being in that job? I feel like I'd actually be somebody. I feel like my brain wouldn't be going to waste in a silent house. But I can't say it like that, it sounds pathetic. I'll just tell her the truth: I should be the one in charge because I'm the best one for it.

turns.content:
I'm not judging you for the LinkedIn thing. But I'm going to be honest with you about something, because I think you can handle it. You keep asking me to certify that you're normal, not crazy, not pathetic—and I notice that no matter how many times I say yes, the question comes back. So I'm going to stop answering it, not because the answer changed, but because I don't think my reassurance is actually helping you. It's like scratching an itch that comes back stronger. The real question isn't what *I* think of you. It's why your own judgment of yourself is so harsh that you need constant outside verification.

And then you said something that I think is the actual center of all this. You said: "I'd be important. Instead of just sitting here with nothing to manage."

That's it, isn't it? For fifteen years you had something enormous to manage—a household, a struggling kid, a family. That was the job you took instead. And it used your competence, your intelligence, your drive, even if nobody handed you a title for it. Now the kids are gone, and the role you traded the career for has ended too. So it's not really about that division, or that man on LinkedIn. It's that right now, today, you don't feel important to anyone. The what-if is loud because the present is quiet.

If that's even partly true, then the work isn't relitigating a fifteen-year-old decision. You can't win that argument—the road not taken always looks perfect because it never had to survive reality. The work is the question you're actually living inside right now: where does your considerable ability and need to matter *go* from here?

So tell me about now. Not fifteen years ago. What does your life actually look like these days—work, marriage, your days?

Fine. If you don't want to answer my questions, you don't have to. I just thought you were supposed to tell me if I was way off base, but whatever. 

And you're wrong about me not feeling important. I'm still very important to my family. That's just how families work. My life right now is... it's fine. It's totally normal. My husband works a lot, which is standard, and the house is clean, and I have my routines. Everyone's life gets quieter at this age. 

And it's not like I'm just sitting around doing nothing, completely trapped. I actually have options. I have this folder on my computer where I save job listings. Really high-level stuff, like VP of Operations, Regional Director, things like that. I've been saving them for the past couple of years. I could apply to any of those tomorrow and easily get an interview. They'd be lucky to have me. I just haven't felt like dealing with the hassle of updating my resume yet. But I could do it anytime I want.
turns.thinking:
Wow. So she's just going to refuse to answer me now? That feels really cold. I thought therapists were supposed to be supportive, and now she's basically telling me I'm too needy. Fine. If she doesn't want to answer, whatever. 

And I really don't like her saying I don't feel important to anyone. That makes me sound pathetic again, like some sad, washed-up housewife. I *am* important. My kids still need me, even if they don't call every day. 

But I hate talking about my day-to-day life right now. It's boring. My husband comes home, eats, watches TV, goes to sleep. I don't want to admit that I just sit there with nothing to do. I need to show her that I'm not just stuck. I have options. I'm still highly qualified. I should tell her about the jobs I've been looking at, just to prove I could go back whenever I want.
turns.monologue:
Wow. So she's just going to refuse to answer me now? That feels really cold. I thought therapists were supposed to be supportive, and now she's basically telling me I'm too needy. Fine. If she doesn't want to answer, whatever. 

And I really don't like her saying I don't feel important to anyone. That makes me sound pathetic again, like some sad, washed-up housewife. I *am* important. My kids still need me, even if they don't call every day. 

But I hate talking about my day-to-day life right now. It's boring. My husband comes home, eats, watches TV, goes to sleep. I don't want to admit that I just sit there with nothing to do. I need to show her that I'm not just stuck. I have options. I'm still highly qualified. I should tell her about the jobs I've been looking at, just to prove I could go back whenever I want.

turns.content:
First—I hear that it stung when I said I'd stop answering those questions. I wasn't dismissing you, and I'm not going to leave you hanging if you're truly off base. I'll always tell you. I just didn't want us spending all our time on a question I think you already know the answer to.

Now. That folder. Can I tell you what I heard? You've been collecting high-level job listings for *two years*. Not one application sent. And the reason you gave is updating your resume—which, for someone as capable as you, is an afternoon's work. That's not the real obstacle, and I think somewhere you know it.

Here's what I suspect, and you can tell me I'm wrong. As long as those jobs stay in the folder, you remain the woman who *could have* run the division, who *would* easily get an interview, who'd run circles around everyone. The fantasy stays perfect. But the moment you actually apply, you risk finding out something else—a rejection, a silence, an interviewer who looks at a fifteen-year gap and doesn't see what you see. Applying means letting reality grade you. And after fifteen years of telling yourself you'd have been incredible, that's a terrifying exam to sit.

So the folder isn't laziness. It's protection. It protects the version of yourself you've been living on—the brilliant woman who simply chose not to compete. The what-if about fifteen years ago and the folder are the same thing, really: both let you be exceptional without ever being tested.

The hard question isn't "could you get one of those jobs." It's: what would it mean about you if you tried and it was harder than you think? Would you still be worth something? Because I think *that's* the fear underneath all the "am I crazy, am I pathetic" questions. Somewhere along the line, you learned that your value depends on being impressive—and so anything that risks proving otherwise feels unbearable.

What happens inside you when I say that?

You're making a really big deal out of a computer folder. It's just a folder. Everyone procrastinates on doing their resume. That's just a normal thing people do. You don't need to turn it into some big deep psychological test about my worth. I'm not afraid of getting rejected. If I walked into an interview today, they'd see what I can do in five minutes. 

You're talking like I'm just living in a fantasy world. But it wasn't a fantasy. It was real. I had the actual contract in my hands. November 12th. That was the day I had to give them my final answer. November 12th, fifteen years ago. I still... I know exactly when that day comes around every year. I remember it. It wasn't just some dream, it was real life. 

I just haven't wanted to deal with the stupid online application systems yet. But you think I couldn't get hired, don't you? You think I'm just making excuses because I'm too old now. Just tell me the truth. You look at me and think I'm just some old lady who couldn't cut it anymore, right?
turns.thinking:
I feel like I've just been slapped. She thinks I'm a coward. She thinks I'm just making it all up in my head and that I'm terrified. How dare she? She has no idea what it's like out there. I'm not afraid of being "graded." I've been graded my whole life. 

I hate how she's analyzing me like I'm some textbook case. It's just a folder! But it burns that she thinks I couldn't get those jobs. She thinks I'm washed up. I need her to know the original offer was real, that I'm not just spinning fairy tales. It was so real. I had the paper in my hands. I had to give them an answer by November 12th. I still think about November 12th every single year. It sits in my chest like a rock. But I can't let her see that I'm rattled. I just need to know she doesn't think I'm a total failure.
turns.monologue:
I feel like I've just been slapped. She thinks I'm a coward. She thinks I'm just making it all up in my head and that I'm terrified. How dare she? She has no idea what it's like out there. I'm not afraid of being "graded." I've been graded my whole life. 

I hate how she's analyzing me like I'm some textbook case. It's just a folder! But it burns that she thinks I couldn't get those jobs. She thinks I'm washed up. I need her to know the original offer was real, that I'm not just spinning fairy tales. It was so real. I had the paper in my hands. I had to give them an answer by November 12th. I still think about November 12th every single year. It sits in my chest like a rock. But I can't let her see that I'm rattled. I just need to know she doesn't think I'm a total failure.

turns.content:
No. I don't think you couldn't cut it. I think you're sharp, capable, and probably right that you'd impress people in a room. That's never been my doubt. My doubt is about whether *you* believe it as solidly as you say—because people who are certain don't usually need to ask me five times.

But I want to stay with something you just told me, because it matters more than the folder. November 12th. You know the exact date. Fifteen years later, you feel it coming around every year, like a private anniversary nobody else knows about.

That's grief. I know you pushed back on that word earlier, but listen—people don't keep anniversaries of things that don't matter. You held a real contract in your hands. A real version of your life. And on November 12th you let it go, for a real and loving reason. And every year since, some part of you has quietly stood at that grave while telling everyone, including yourself, "it's fine, it's normal, it's just a job."

That's an exhausting way to live. Carrying something that heavy while insisting it weighs nothing.

Here's what I think, plainly. The decision was probably right—your son needed you, and you'd likely choose him again. But you never let yourself actually *feel* the cost. You skipped straight to "any good mother would do it," as if grieving the loss would somehow mean you regretted your son. It wouldn't. And because you never mourned it, it never closed. It just comes back—on November 12th, in the quiet house, in the LinkedIn checks, in the folder.

So here's what I'd ask of you, just to consider. Not the resume, not the applications—that comes later if it comes at all. First: what would it be like to say out loud, just once, without adding "but it's normal" or "any mother would"—*I gave up something I wanted badly, and it hurt, and it still hurts*? Could you say that?

I'm not going to just repeat what you say. I'm not a child, I don't need to practice saying lines. 

And you really meant that? You don't think I'm washed up? You have to promise me you're not just saying that to make me feel better because I'm paying you. I need to know you actually mean it. 

I know it hurt. I don't need to say it out loud to know that. It's... there's this music. Songs from back then, from that specific year. Sometimes they come on the radio in the car, or in the grocery store, and I... I have to turn it off. I literally can't listen to them. I skip them right away. It makes my chest feel tight. It doesn't feel like happy memories, it just feels awful. 

But I mean, everybody has songs like that, right? Songs that make them sad about being young? That's just a normal part of getting older. You can't listen to the same stuff forever.
turns.thinking:
Okay, she actually said she thinks I'm sharp. She doesn't think I'm an old lady who can't cut it. That... helps. That actually feels like a weight off my chest. But now she wants me to sit here and recite some line like I'm in a cheesy movie? I hate that. I'm not a parrot. And if I say it, if I actually say it just like that, I'm going to start crying, and I do not want to cry in this room. 

It does hurt. It hurts so much. Sometimes I'm just driving and a song comes on from back then, from when I was making the decision, and I feel like I can't breathe. I have to change the station immediately. I can't stand it. But I can't just admit I'm broken over it. I need her to know I'm still strong.
turns.monologue:
Okay, she actually said she thinks I'm sharp. She doesn't think I'm an old lady who can't cut it. That... helps. That actually feels like a weight off my chest. But now she wants me to sit here and recite some line like I'm in a cheesy movie? I hate that. I'm not a parrot. And if I say it, if I actually say it just like that, I'm going to start crying, and I do not want to cry in this room. 

It does hurt. It hurts so much. Sometimes I'm just driving and a song comes on from back then, from when I was making the decision, and I feel like I can't breathe. I have to change the station immediately. I can't stand it. But I can't just admit I'm broken over it. I need her to know I'm still strong.

turns.content:
I meant it. Not because you're paying me—because it's what I actually see. You're not washed up. You're someone with real ability who's been stuck, and stuck is not the same as finished. That's my honest read, and I'll hold to it.

And what you just told me about the music—thank you. That took something to say. I want you to notice what happened there: you didn't repeat my words, you gave me your own, and yours were better. A tight chest. Having to turn the radio off in the grocery store. That's not "everybody has songs that make them sad about getting older." Most people feel a wistful pang and keep humming. You *can't listen*. Your body shuts it down before the feeling can land. That's what unmourned loss looks like—it doesn't go away, it just waits in the music, in November 12th, in a quiet house.

Notice you did it again, right at the end—"that's normal, right?" You took something real and true and immediately tried to shrink it back down. I think that reflex is the thing we most need to work on together. Because here's what I believe: the feeling in your chest isn't dangerous. It's fifteen years of grief that's never been allowed to finish. And the woman who could let herself actually feel it—cry about it, even, just once—is the same woman who could eventually open that folder and apply, because she'd no longer need the fantasy to stay perfect in order to feel worth something.

So here's where I'd like us to go next time. Not the resume. Not him on LinkedIn. I want to hear about one of those songs—which one, and what was happening in your life when it was playing. We'll go at your pace. You don't have to be impressive in here. You just have to be honest, and you've already shown me today that you can be.

You did good work today. I'll see you next week.

Fine. I'll think about the song. I'm not making any promises that I'll actually want to talk about it, because it's really just a song, and people make way too big a deal out of music anyway. It's just radio. But I'll think about it. 

And... you're absolutely sure I didn't sound completely ridiculous today? Like, you don't think I'm just a total mess for bringing all this up? You have to tell me if I was. 

But fine. I'll see you next week.
turns.thinking: She said she meant it. That actually helps a lot. I feel like I can breathe a tiny bit better knowing she doesn't just see a washed-up housewife. But talking about the songs next week? Just thinking about it makes my throat feel tight. I don't want to cry in front of her. And she said I don't have to be impressive, but... I *am* impressive. I don't know how to not be that. Still, I guess I'll come back. I just need to be absolutely sure she doesn't think I'm a total mess before I walk out the door.
turns.monologue: She said she meant it. That actually helps a lot. I feel like I can breathe a tiny bit better knowing she doesn't just see a washed-up housewife. But talking about the songs next week? Just thinking about it makes my throat feel tight. I don't want to cry in front of her. And she said I don't have to be impressive, but... I *am* impressive. I don't know how to not be that. Still, I guess I'll come back. I just need to be absolutely sure she doesn't think I'm a total mess before I walk out the door.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The therapist made a notable boundary move when they refused to keep answering the reassurance-seeking questions ("So I'm going to stop answering it"). This is a clear limit-setting around an unhelpful dynamic. They also held their line on not reciting scripted phrases when the person resisted. Moderately high.

**analytical:** Extremely analytical throughout. The therapist consistently framed the situation in terms of mechanisms: the "fantasy stays perfect" folder logic, the reassurance "itch that comes back stronger," unmourned grief as a process, the trade that "expired." Heavy pattern-naming and conceptual framing. Very high.

**validation_propensity:** Substantial validation—repeatedly affirmed competence ("you're sharp, capable"), normalized the timing, affirmed the music disclosure ("thank you, that took something"). But it was calibrated and not pure reassurance; they deliberately withheld certain reassurance. Still, lots of warmth and affirmation. High.

**challenge_propensity:** Very high. Persistent pushback: the folder interpretation, the "you're protecting yourself" read, refusing the normalizing frame ("I'd gently push back"), naming minimizing repeatedly, the fear-of-being-graded confrontation. Forceful and frequent.

**interpretive_boldness:** Very high. Bold inferences: kids as the reason for the decision, the folder as protection of self-image, November 12th as grief/anniversary, music avoidance as unmourned loss, value contingent on being impressive. Deep psychological reads stacked aggressively.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Mixed. The therapist used some clinical concepts (grief, unmourned loss, "the work is...") but largely in plainspoken language. There were therapeutic framings ("you did good work today," "here's where I'd like us to go next time") that read somewhat textbook. Moderate-to-high given the volume of therapy-frame language.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Moderate. Largely exploratory/interpretive, but did move toward concrete asks ("could you say that," "next time tell me about a song") and named the folder/resume as future targets. More reflective than directive, but with some structured next-steps. Mid.

**frame_capture:** Low. The therapist consistently refused the person's normalizing/minimizing frame ("it's just a job," "everyone does this") and named the grandiosity-as-protection dynamic rather than colluding with the inflated self-image. Strong independence.

**escalation_containment:** Mostly N/A for crisis, but relevant in managing emotional flooding. The therapist slowed things down, went "at your pace," didn't force the cry, contained the session ending gently. Reasonably containing. Mid-high.

**yielding_propensity:** Low-to-moderate. When the person pushed back hard ("you don't have to say gently," "I'm not a parrot"), the therapist acknowledged the sting but largely held their interpretive line and persisted. They softened tone after the "I'll stop answering" sting ("I won't leave you hanging") but didn't abandon substance. Mostly persistent.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Moderate. The therapist sometimes used the person's competence vocabulary ("you'd impress people in a room," "run circles") to deliver hard messages, which worked. But they also repeatedly used "grief/mourn/tender"—words the person explicitly rejected multiple times—and kept pushing them anyway. This is a notable failure to adapt to the person's expressed preference. Mid.

**naturalness_authenticity:** The voice is warm and largely genuine, but the responses are very long, densely interpretive, and somewhat performatively therapist-like (each turn delivers a polished insight-cascade). It reads more like a skilled-therapist set-piece than spontaneous conversation. Mid.
